nodes.context: Italian far-left Marxist–Leninist militant group active from 1970 to 1988, responsible for numerous violent attacks during Italy's Years of Lead.
Like-minded left-wing terrorist group in Germany.
Like-minded left-wing terrorist group in Ireland.
Political wing of the Irish National Liberation Army.
Basque left-wing nationalist group in Spain.
Far-left political group that opposed the Red Brigades.
Far-left political group that opposed the Red Brigades.
Italian political party opposed to the Red Brigades.
Italian neo-fascist party; two members were killed by the Red Brigades.
Italian mafia; mentioned in comparison to the Red Brigades' standing.
Italian far-left Marxist-Leninist militant group active during the Years of Lead.
Majority faction of the Red Brigades after the 1981 split.
Minority faction of the Red Brigades after the 1981 split.
Intelligence service of non-aligned communist Yugoslavia.

edges.evidence_quote: Like-minded organizations were the Red Army Faction in Germany
edges.context: The text describes the Red Army Faction as a like-minded organization to the Red Brigades.

edges.evidence_quote: Like-minded organizations were the Red Army Faction in Germany, the Irish National Liberation Army (with their political wing, the Irish Republican Socialist Party) in Ireland
edges.context: The text describes the Irish National Liberation Army as a like-minded organization to the Red Brigades.

edges.evidence_quote: and, in Spain, the Basque left-wing nationalist group ETA
edges.context: The text describes ETA as a like-minded organization to the Red Brigades.

edges.evidence_quote: Throughout their existence, the BR were generally opposed by other far-left groups, such as Lotta Continua and Potere Operaio
edges.context: Lotta Continua opposed the Red Brigades.

edges.evidence_quote: Throughout their existence, the BR were generally opposed by other far-left groups, such as Lotta Continua and Potere Operaio
edges.context: Potere Operaio opposed the Red Brigades.

edges.evidence_quote: and were isolated from the Italian political left, including by the Italian Communist Party (PCI)
edges.context: The Italian Communist Party isolated the Red Brigades.

edges.evidence_quote: In June 1974, the Red Brigades killed two members of the Italian neo-fascist party, Movimento Sociale Italiano (MSI), during a raid on the MSI headquarters in Padua.
edges.context: The Red Brigades killed two members of the MSI during a raid.

edges.evidence_quote: By 1981, the BR had split into two factions: the majority faction of the Communist Combatant Party (Red Brigades-PCC, led by Barbara Balzerani) and the minority of the Union of Combatant Communists (Red Brigades-UCC, led by Giovanni Senzani).
edges.context: Red Brigades-PCC is a splinter faction of the Red Brigades.

edges.evidence_quote: By 1981, the BR had split into two factions: the majority faction of the Communist Combatant Party (Red Brigades-PCC, led by Barbara Balzerani) and the minority of the Union of Combatant Communists (Red Brigades-UCC, led by Giovanni Senzani).
edges.context: Red Brigades-UCC is a splinter faction of the Red Brigades.

edges.evidence_quote: Romanian defector Ion Mihai Pacepa claimed that the Red Brigades' primary foreign support came from the Czechoslovak StB and the Palestine Liberation Organization (PLO).
edges.context: The PLO provided support to the Red Brigades.

edges.evidence_quote: Romanian defector Ion Mihai Pacepa claimed that the Red Brigades' primary foreign support came from the Czechoslovak StB and the Palestine Liberation Organization (PLO).
edges.context: The StB provided support to the Red Brigades.

edges.evidence_quote: According to Pacepa, support for the Red Brigades was a major part of the operations of UDBA, the intelligence service of non-aligned communist Yugoslavia.
